name: KSU ASCEND Program
summary: ASCEND supports KSU students with foster care or homelessness experience through campus-based retention help.
eligibility: KSU students with foster care or homelessness experience.
address: Kennesaw State University, Kennesaw and Marietta, Georgia

name: KSU CARE Services
summary: KSU CARE Services helps students facing housing, food, and emergency basic-needs barriers.
eligibility: KSU students needing basic-needs or short-term crisis support.
address: Kennesaw State University, Kennesaw and Marietta, Georgia

name: Embark Georgia Campus Programs
summary: Embark Georgia connects students with campus support programs, scholarships, and college persistence help.
eligibility: Georgia students with foster care or homelessness experience seeking postsecondary support.

name: Embark Georgia - Chafee ETV
summary: Embark Georgia administers Chafee ETV funding for Georgia youth with qualifying foster care history.
eligibility: Youth ages 18 to 25 with qualifying foster care history and education or training plans.

name: Connected By 21
summary: Connected By 21 organizes Georgia supports for youth aging out, including housing, life skills, and transition planning.
eligibility: Georgia youth transitioning from foster care who need post-18 support planning.

name: Georgia DFCS Youth Information
summary: Georgia DFCS Youth Information highlights transition resources, youth programs, and foster-care planning supports.
eligibility: Georgia youth in care or transitioning out of care.

name: Georgia CREW
summary: Georgia CREW helps youth in care build work readiness, career exposure, and employment skills.
eligibility: Georgia youth in foster care, generally ages 14 to 21, seeking workforce preparation.

name: SNAP Works for Former Foster Youth
summary: SNAP Works can connect eligible former foster youth to training, credentials, and employment support.
eligibility: Former foster youth who meet SNAP Works program criteria in participating areas.

name: Wellroot Family Services
summary: Wellroot supports youth transitioning from foster care with housing-focused and wraparound services.
eligibility: Transition-age youth needing housing support, especially youth leaving foster care.

name: 211 Georgia
summary: 211 Georgia connects people to local housing, food, crisis, health, and community services.
eligibility: Anyone in Georgia needing help finding community resources.

name: Georgia DFCS Emergency Support
summary: Georgia DFCS provides emergency child welfare and foster-care contact pathways for urgent family or youth safety needs.
eligibility: Georgia residents needing urgent child welfare or foster-care support.

name: Georgia Medicaid Extension for Former Foster Youth
summary: Georgia extends Medicaid for many youth who were in foster care at age 18.
eligibility: Youth who were in foster care at age 18 may qualify for health coverage through age 26.

name: Extended Youth Support Services (EYSS)
summary: EYSS is part of Georgia's extended foster-care support pathway for eligible youth who aged out at 18.
eligibility: Youth who aged out at 18 and meet Georgia extended foster-care participation rules.

name: Cobb Homeless Alliance
summary: Cobb Homeless Alliance coordinates housing providers and homelessness response resources in Cobb County.
eligibility: Cobb County residents experiencing homelessness or housing instability.

name: MUST Ministries
summary: MUST Ministries offers emergency assistance, shelter-linked support, food, and basic-needs help in metro Atlanta.
eligibility: People in crisis needing food, housing support, or basic-needs assistance.

name: Atlanta Legal Aid - Cobb County
summary: Atlanta Legal Aid's Cobb office helps eligible residents with housing, family, and public-benefits legal issues.
eligibility: Low-income Cobb County residents with qualifying civil legal needs.

name: Georgia Legal Services Program
summary: Georgia Legal Services Program provides civil legal help to eligible Georgians outside metro Atlanta coverage areas.
eligibility: Low-income Georgians with qualifying civil legal problems.

name: Georgia Gateway
summary: Georgia Gateway is the main online portal for Medicaid, SNAP, TANF, and other state-administered benefits.
eligibility: Georgia residents applying for public benefits or managing an existing benefits case.

name: Opening a Checking or Savings Account in Georgia
summary: To open a checking or savings account in Georgia you need a valid photo ID (Georgia state ID, driver's license, or US passport), your Social Security number or ITIN, and proof of current address such as a lease, utility bill, or mail from a government agency. Banks do not run a credit check to open a standard checking or savings account, so a limited or missing credit history does not block you — which is why these accounts are a good fit for foster youth, former foster youth, and other transition-age young adults. Credit unions such as Georgia's Own and Delta Community routinely open accounts for young adults and former foster youth with no credit history. Bank On certified accounts, available at many Georgia banks and credit unions, are designed for people who have been unbanked or underbanked; they have no overdraft fees and a low minimum opening deposit (often $25 or less), which makes them especially useful for youth leaving foster care. If you do not have a current address on file, a DFCS case manager or transitional housing program can help you document a mailing address.
eligibility: Anyone 18 or older with valid ID and a Social Security number or ITIN. Minors may open joint accounts with a trusted adult.

name: Georgia's Own Credit Union
summary: Georgia's Own is a member-owned credit union open to anyone who lives, works, worships, or attends school in Georgia. They offer student checking, basic checking, and savings accounts with no monthly fee for eligible accounts and no credit check required to open a standard account. Membership requires a $5 opening deposit into a savings account.
eligibility: Anyone who lives, works, worships, or attends school in Georgia. $5 minimum opens membership.

name: Replacing a Social Security Card in Georgia
summary: To replace a lost or stolen Social Security card, apply through the Social Security Administration (SSA). Replacement cards are free. You need one primary proof of identity — a U.S. driver's license, state-issued ID card, or U.S. passport is preferred. If you don't have any of those, SSA may accept two secondary documents such as an employer ID, school ID, health insurance card (not Medicare), or military ID. You also need to show proof of U.S. citizenship or lawful immigration status if that isn't already on file with SSA; a certified U.S. birth certificate works for citizenship. If you already have a my Social Security online account and a U.S. mailing address, you can usually request the replacement at ssa.gov without visiting an office. Otherwise, apply in person at any Georgia SSA field office. SSA limits replacements to three per year and ten in a lifetime, though replacements tied to a legal name change don't count against those limits. A new card typically arrives in the mail within 10 to 14 business days. If you don't have a photo ID, ask your DFCS case manager — they can often help you get one or write a verification letter the SSA will accept in place of some ID documents.
eligibility: Anyone with a Social Security number needing a replacement card. U.S. citizens and lawful residents.

name: Getting a Georgia State ID or Driver's License as a Foster Youth
summary: The Georgia Department of Driver Services (DDS) issues state ID cards and driver's licenses. Youth currently in foster care and former foster youth who meet DDS eligibility rules can qualify for a fee waiver, meaning the ID or license is issued at no cost. To apply, bring one primary proof of identity (certified U.S. birth certificate, U.S. passport, or permanent resident card), proof of your Social Security number (Social Security card, W-2, or 1099), and two proofs of Georgia residency (lease, utility bill, bank statement, school record, or a letter from a group home or placement provider). Foster youth applying for the fee waiver should also bring documentation of foster-care status — a verification letter from your DFCS case manager is the standard document, and DDS can confirm what they accept at your service center. Apply in person at any Georgia DDS Customer Service Center; most centers issue a temporary paper license the same day and mail the plastic card within 2 to 4 weeks. If you do not yet have a certified birth certificate, get one first — Georgia DPH Vital Records issues them, and a DFCS case manager can help request it on your behalf.
eligibility: Georgia residents. Fee waiver: youth currently in foster care or former foster youth meeting DDS eligibility criteria. Ask your DFCS case manager for a verification letter.

name: Getting a Georgia Birth Certificate
summary: Georgia birth certificates are issued by the Georgia Department of Public Health (DPH) State Office of Vital Records. A certified copy typically costs $25, with additional copies ordered at the same time at $5 each — fees are set by DPH and can change, so check the Vital Records website for current pricing before you go. You can order the certificate if you are the person named on the record (once you are 18 or older), a parent listed on the record, a spouse, adult child, sibling, grandparent, or a legal guardian with proof of relationship. For youth in foster care, a DFCS case manager has the authority to request a birth certificate on your behalf, and they do this routinely as part of Independent Living Program transition services when youth need vital records for school, benefits, or ID applications — so yes, a caseworker can pull it for you. You can order in person at the State Office of Vital Records in Atlanta or at any Georgia county vital records office (usually fastest, often same-day), by mail using the DPH vital records application form (4 to 6 weeks), or online through the authorized vendor VitalChek (7 to 10 business days, with additional vendor fees). If you were born outside Georgia, Georgia cannot issue another state's birth certificate — you must contact the vital records office of the state where you were born. The CDC's National Center for Health Statistics publishes a directory of every state's vital records office, and a DFCS case manager can help you navigate that. To order, you need a valid photo ID (state ID, driver's license, or U.S. passport). If you do not have a photo ID yet, a DFCS case manager can submit the request on your behalf or help you get an ID first.
eligibility: Person on the record (age 18+), immediate family with proof of relationship, legal guardian or custodian, or a DFCS case manager acting on behalf of a youth in care.
services: DFCS case manager assistance for youth in care
source_name: Georgia Department of Public Health Vital Records

name: FDIC Money Smart for Young People
summary: FDIC Money Smart for Young People is a free, plain-language curriculum with modules on earning, spending, saving, borrowing, and protecting money. It does not require an account or sign-up. Topics include how to build a monthly budget, how to track spending against income, how checking and savings accounts work, how to read a bank statement, and how to avoid common financial scams. It is the federal government's standard financial-literacy resource for teens and young adults.
eligibility: Free and open to anyone. No account required.
